title: Civil Procedure
subject: Civil Procedure
summary: Civil Procedure covers federal jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction, venue, Erie, pleadings, joinder, discovery, trial motions, appeals, and preclusion.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Civil Procedure is about where a lawsuit may be filed, how the lawsuit moves through court, and when a court’s decision becomes final.
The main MBE questions usually ask:
Does this court have power, did the parties follow the correct procedure, and what happens next?
This outline was reviewed against the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure effective December 1, 2025.
The 2025 amendments affected Rules 16 and 26 and added Rule 16.1 for multidistrict litigation.
Rules involving specialized courts, multidistrict litigation, abstention, and uncommon federal statutes are included but marked as lower priority when appropriate.

sections.title: The Three Questions to Ask First
body: The Three Questions to Ask First
Whenever a lawsuit is filed in federal court, begin with three separate questions:
Does the court have subject matter jurisdiction?
Does the court have personal jurisdiction over the defendant?
Subject matter jurisdiction asks whether this type of case belongs in federal court.
Personal jurisdiction asks whether this defendant can be forced to defend in this state.
Venue asks which federal district is the proper location.
Think of a federal courthouse like a concert venue.
Subject matter jurisdiction asks: Is this type of event allowed in the building?
Personal jurisdiction asks: Can this particular performer be required to appear?
Venue asks: Is this the correct branch or location?
Power over the case, power over the defendant, proper geographic location.

sections.title: Subject Matter Jurisdiction
body: Subject matter jurisdiction is the court’s power to hear the type of dispute before it.
Federal courts are courts of limited jurisdiction.
A federal court must have a specific constitutional and statutory basis for hearing the case.
Subject matter jurisdiction cannot be created by agreement, consent, or waiver.
A party or the court may raise a lack of subject matter jurisdiction at any time, including on appeal.
Priya and Owen agree in a contract that any future disagreement will be heard in federal court.
Their agreement cannot create federal subject matter jurisdiction.
The case must still qualify under federal question, diversity, or another jurisdictional statute.
The parties cannot give a federal court power that Congress did not give it.

sections.title: Abstention and Limited Federal Intervention
body: A federal court that has jurisdiction usually must exercise it.
In narrow situations, the court may delay or decline federal adjudication in deference to state proceedings.
The federal constitutional issue may disappear if a state court first resolves an uncertain issue of state law.
Federal involvement would interfere with a complex state administrative or regulatory system involving strong state interests.
A federal court generally avoids interfering with certain ongoing state criminal, quasi-criminal, or contempt proceedings.
Exceptional circumstances may justify staying or dismissing a federal case because of parallel state litigation.
Mere duplication or inconvenience is ordinarily not enough.
Federal intervention may be appropriate when there is:
Immediate and irreparable injury that cannot be addressed through the state proceeding
A company asks a federal court to stop an ongoing state environmental-enforcement action.
Because the proceeding is coercive, important to the state, and already underway, abstention may be appropriate unless the company can show extraordinary bad faith or irreparable injury.
Federal courts usually exercise jurisdiction, but extraordinary state interests or proceedings may justify abstention.

sections.title: Transfer When the Federal Court Lacks Jurisdiction
body: Transfer When the Federal Court Lacks Jurisdiction
When a federal court lacks jurisdiction, it may transfer the action to another federal court where the action could have been brought if transfer is in the interest of justice.
The transferred action is generally treated as filed in the new court on the original filing date.
A claimant timely files in the wrong federal court because a specialized statute gives exclusive jurisdiction to another federal court.
Rather than dismissing after the limitations period has expired, the first court may transfer the case in the interest of justice.
Transfer moves a case between federal courts.
Removal moves a case from state court to federal court.
Remand sends a removed case back to state court.
Wrong federal court may transfer; state-to-federal movement is removal.

sections.title: Specialized Federal Courts
body: Congress has created specialized federal courts with limited subject matter jurisdiction.
U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit
A taxpayer challenges an IRS deficiency determination in the U.S. Tax Court.
The case rests on the Tax Court’s specialized statutory jurisdiction rather than ordinary federal question or diversity jurisdiction.
Some disputes go to a specialized federal court because Congress assigned that category there.

sections.title: Federal Question Jurisdiction
body: Federal question jurisdiction exists when the plaintiff’s claim arises under:
There is no minimum amount in controversy.
A city employee sues the city under a federal civil-rights statute.
The claim was created by federal law.
The federal court has federal question jurisdiction.
Federal right in the plaintiff’s claim = federal question jurisdiction.

sections.title: Concurrent and Exclusive Federal Jurisdiction
body: Most federal claims may be heard in either state court or federal court.
Congress sometimes gives only federal courts power over a category of cases.
Harper brings an ordinary federal employment-discrimination claim in state court.
That may be allowed because state and federal courts generally have concurrent jurisdiction over the claim.
Federal law does not automatically mean federal court only.

sections.title: The Well-Pleaded Complaint Rule
body: Federal question jurisdiction must appear on the face of the plaintiff’s properly pleaded complaint.
The court looks only at the plaintiff’s own claim.
A landlord brings a state-law eviction claim.
The tenant plans to argue that the eviction violates federal housing law.
The anticipated federal defense does not create federal question jurisdiction.
The federal issue must be in the plaintiff’s claim, not waiting in the defendant’s defense.

sections.title: State Claims Containing Federal Issues
body: A state-law claim may sometimes create federal question jurisdiction if the federal issue is:
Capable of federal resolution without disrupting the federal-state balance
A state quiet-title claim turns entirely on whether a federal tax notice was legally sufficient.
Because the federal issue is necessary, disputed, and important to the federal system, federal question jurisdiction may exist.
A state claim enters federal court only when the embedded federal issue is genuinely central and substantial.

sections.title: Declaratory Judgment Actions
body: A declaratory judgment action does not create federal jurisdiction by itself.
The court asks what coercive lawsuit would have been brought without the request for declaratory relief.
A publisher expects an author to sue for breach of a state-law licensing agreement.
The publisher files first and asks a federal court to declare that no breach occurred.
The threatened action is a state contract claim, so the declaratory action does not create federal question jurisdiction.
For declaratory relief, imagine the lawsuit that would otherwise happen.

sections.title: Diversity Jurisdiction
body: An amount in controversy that exceeds $75,000
The claim itself may arise under state law.
Elena, a citizen of Colorado, sues Trent, a citizen of Nevada, for $120,000 under state negligence law.
Complete diversity exists, and the amount exceeds $75,000.
The federal court may exercise diversity jurisdiction.
Different states on opposite sides + more than $75,000.

sections.title: Complete Diversity
body: Complete diversity means that no plaintiff may be a citizen of the same state as any defendant.
Plaintiffs may share citizenship with each other.
Defendants may share citizenship with each other.
The problem occurs when citizenship crosses opposing sides.
Complete diversity is destroyed because Plaintiff 1 and Defendant 2 are both citizens of Maine.
No plaintiff may match any defendant.

sections.title: Minimal Diversity and Party Realignment
body: Minimal diversity exists when at least one plaintiff and one defendant are citizens of different states.
Ordinary diversity jurisdiction requires complete diversity, but Congress permits minimal diversity in limited situations.
Certain mass-accident cases under federal statute
The court looks beyond the caption and aligns parties according to their real interests in the dispute.
A shareholder names a corporation as a defendant in a derivative suit, but the corporation will receive the recovery if the suit succeeds.
The court may realign the corporation as a plaintiff when testing diversity.
Ordinary diversity is complete; special statutes sometimes accept minimal diversity, and courts align parties by real interests.

sections.title: When Diversity Is Measured
body: Diversity is measured when the lawsuit is filed.
Later changes in citizenship generally do not destroy jurisdiction that properly existed at filing.
A citizen of Georgia sues a citizen of Alabama in federal court.
Two months later, the plaintiff moves to Alabama permanently.
The later move does not destroy diversity jurisdiction.

sections.title: Foreign Sovereigns and Direct-Action Insurers
body: A foreign state is generally immune from suit unless a statutory exception applies.
Commercial activity connected to the United States
Certain torts committed in the United States
Direct Action Against a Liability Insurer
In a direct action against an insurer where the insured is not joined, the insurer is treated as a citizen of:
Every state where the insurer is independently a citizen, and
This special rule does not apply to an ordinary contract suit by the insured against its own insurer.
A state statute lets an injured pedestrian sue a delivery company’s insurer directly without joining the delivery company.
For diversity, the insurer may also take the delivery company’s citizenship.
Foreign states may be immune, and a direct-action insurer may inherit the insured’s citizenship.

sections.title: Citizenship of Individuals
body: An individual is a citizen of the state where the individual is domiciled.
Physical presence in the state, and
A person may have many residences but only one domicile at a time.
Devon rents an apartment in Arizona for a six-month work assignment but plans to return permanently to New Mexico.
Devon remains domiciled in New Mexico.
Domicile = presence + intent to stay indefinitely.

sections.title: Changing Domicile
body: A person changes domicile only by:
Moving to a new state, and
Intent without movement is not enough.
Movement without intent is not enough.
Sienna moves from Ohio to Washington, signs a long-term lease, accepts permanent employment, and registers to vote there.
She likely changed her domicile to Washington.
You must actually arrive and genuinely plan to stay.

sections.title: U.S. Citizens Living Abroad
body: A U.S. citizen domiciled in a foreign country is generally considered stateless for diversity purposes.
That person is not a citizen of a U.S. state and is not treated as a foreign citizen.
A U.S. citizen permanently domiciled in Spain sues a citizen of Texas.
Ordinary diversity jurisdiction generally does not exist because the plaintiff is not a citizen of any U.S. state.
An American permanently domiciled abroad may fall into the diversity gap.

sections.title: Citizenship of Corporations
body: A corporation is a citizen of:
Every state or foreign country in which it is incorporated, and
The one state or foreign country where it has its principal place of business
The principal place of business is usually the corporation’s nerve center.
The nerve center is where high-level officers direct, control, and coordinate corporate activity.
It is often the corporate headquarters.
A corporation is incorporated in Delaware.
Its executives direct the company from its headquarters in Utah.
The corporation is a citizen of Delaware and Utah.

sections.title: Citizenship of Partnerships and LLCs
body: An unincorporated association is a citizen of every state where any member is a citizen.
For a limited partnership, count both general and limited partners.
For an LLC, count every member.
An LLC has three members domiciled in Idaho, Montana, and Wyoming.
The LLC is a citizen of all three states.
For an LLC or partnership, trace every member.

sections.title: Representatives and Estates
body: The legal representative of a decedent’s estate takes the decedent’s citizenship.
A representative of a minor or incompetent person takes that person’s citizenship.
An executor lives in Florida, but the decedent was domiciled in Tennessee.
For diversity purposes, the executor is treated as a citizen of Tennessee.
The representative carries the represented person’s citizenship.

sections.title: Foreign Citizens and Diversity
body: A U.S. state citizen and a foreign citizen
Citizens of different U.S. states with foreign citizens added as additional parties
Diversity generally does not exist when:
The only parties are foreign citizens
Foreign citizens appear on both sides while U.S. citizens appear on only one side
A permanent resident is domiciled in the same state as the opposing U.S. citizen
Alienage jurisdiction still requires the correct alignment of U.S. and foreign citizenship.

sections.title: Probate and Domestic Relations Exceptions
body: Federal courts generally do not use diversity jurisdiction to:
A related damages claim may still be heard if it does not require the federal court to perform the core probate or domestic-relations function.
A beneficiary brings a tort claim alleging that an accountant fraudulently diverted estate funds.
The federal court may hear the damages claim if it does not need to probate the will or administer the estate.
Federal courts may hear related disputes, but they do not become divorce or probate courts.

sections.title: Amount in Controversy
body: The amount in controversy must exceed $75,000.
A claim for exactly $75,000 is not enough.
The court excludes ordinary interest and costs.
Attorney’s fees recoverable by statute or contract
A plaintiff seeks $74,000 in compensatory damages and $10,000 in attorney’s fees authorized by statute.
The amount in controversy may exceed $75,000.
The number must be $75,000.01 or more.

sections.title: Legal-Certainty Test
body: The plaintiff’s good-faith allegation controls unless it is legally certain that the plaintiff cannot recover more than $75,000.
A later recovery below the jurisdictional amount does not destroy jurisdiction.
The smaller verdict does not retroactively eliminate diversity jurisdiction.
Good-faith amount at filing controls, not the final verdict.

sections.title: Aggregating One Plaintiff’s Claims
body: One plaintiff may add together all claims against one defendant to exceed $75,000.
The claims do not need to be related.
Both claims are against the same defendant.
The plaintiff may aggregate them to reach $80,000.
One plaintiff versus one defendant may stack all claims.

sections.title: Claims Against Multiple Defendants
body: A plaintiff generally cannot aggregate separate claims against different defendants.
Aggregation is allowed when the defendants are jointly liable for the same obligation.
A plaintiff seeks $50,000 from one driver and $40,000 from a different driver for unrelated accidents.
If both defendants are jointly liable for one $90,000 injury, the full amount may count.
Separate defendants stay separate unless liability is joint.

sections.title: Multiple Plaintiffs and Aggregation
body: Multiple plaintiffs generally cannot aggregate separate claims merely to exceed $75,000.
They may aggregate when enforcing one common and undivided right.
Three owners jointly claim a single $120,000 interest in a trust fund.
They may be able to aggregate because they assert one shared right.
Separate injuries do not combine; one shared right may.

sections.title: Counterclaims and the Diversity Amount
body: A defendant’s counterclaim does not help the plaintiff satisfy the original amount-in-controversy requirement.
A compulsory counterclaim arises from the same transaction or occurrence.
It usually falls within supplemental jurisdiction and does not need to independently exceed $75,000.
It generally needs its own basis for subject matter jurisdiction.
A buyer brings a $90,000 diversity suit over a machinery contract.
The seller asserts a $15,000 compulsory counterclaim for storage expenses arising from the same failed delivery.
The counterclaim may proceed through supplemental jurisdiction.
Compulsory counterclaim rides with the case; unrelated counterclaim needs its own ticket.

sections.title: Devices That Create or Destroy Diversity
body: Devices That Create or Destroy Diversity
A party may not manufacture federal jurisdiction through an improper or collusive assignment.
A genuine assignment makes the assignee the real party in interest, and the assignee’s citizenship generally controls.
Parties cannot create diversity by deliberately omitting a nondiverse person who is indispensable under Rule 19.
A person may genuinely change domicile before filing even when motivated by a desire to create or destroy diversity.
The move and intent to remain must be real.
A substituted party under Rule 25 generally steps into the original party’s position without retesting diversity.
A replacement for an incorrectly named party generally must satisfy jurisdictional requirements.
A claim is assigned to a distant relative for one dollar solely so the relative can sue in federal court and immediately return all proceeds.
The court may disregard the collusive assignment.
A genuine legal change counts; a jurisdictional costume does not.

sections.title: Supplemental Jurisdiction
body: Supplemental jurisdiction allows a federal court with original jurisdiction over one claim to hear additional claims that lack an independent basis of federal jurisdiction.
The claims must arise from a:
The claims should be part of the same constitutional case or controversy.
A worker brings a federal discrimination claim and a state battery claim based on the same confrontation with a supervisor.
The federal court may exercise supplemental jurisdiction over the state claim.
One factual story can bring related claims into federal court together.

sections.title: Supplemental Jurisdiction in Federal Question Cases
body: Supplemental Jurisdiction in Federal Question Cases
When the anchor claim is a federal question, the court may generally hear related state claims against:
The common-nucleus test must be satisfied.
A patient sues a hospital under a federal disability statute.
The patient also asserts a related state negligence claim against the hospital and a nurse arising from the same denial of treatment.
Supplemental jurisdiction may cover both related state claims.
Federal anchor claim can pull in the rest of the same factual dispute.

sections.title: Supplemental Jurisdiction in Diversity Cases
body: In diversity cases, supplemental jurisdiction cannot be used in a way that defeats complete diversity.
Section 1367(b) primarily restricts certain claims by plaintiffs.
When original jurisdiction is based only on diversity, supplemental jurisdiction generally does not cover claims by plaintiffs against parties added under:
if doing so would be inconsistent with diversity requirements.
A California plaintiff sues a Nevada defendant for $200,000.
The Nevada defendant impleads a California contractor.
The defendant’s indemnity claim against the contractor may qualify for supplemental jurisdiction.
The California plaintiff generally cannot use supplemental jurisdiction to add a claim against the California contractor.
In diversity cases, watch the plaintiff trying to reach a nondiverse added party.

if doing so would be inconsistent with diversity requirements.

sections.title: Supplemental Jurisdiction and the Amount Requirement
body: Supplemental Jurisdiction and the Amount Requirement
If one plaintiff has a qualifying diversity claim exceeding $75,000, another diverse plaintiff with a related smaller claim may sometimes join through supplemental jurisdiction.
A Kansas driver sues a Missouri trucking company for $300,000 after a collision.
A Kansas passenger has a related $20,000 claim against the same company.
Because complete diversity remains and the claims arise from the same accident, supplemental jurisdiction may cover the passenger’s claim.
A related smaller claim may ride with a qualifying claim, but citizenship cannot break.

sections.title: Discretion to Decline Supplemental Jurisdiction
body: A federal court may decline supplemental jurisdiction when:
The state issue is novel or complex
All federal claims have been dismissed
A federal claim is dismissed at the beginning of the case, leaving only a difficult and unsettled state constitutional claim.
The court may dismiss or remand the state claim.
Supplemental jurisdiction is power the court may decline to use.

sections.title: Supplemental-Jurisdiction Tolling
body: The statute of limitations for a supplemental state claim is generally tolled:
While the claim is pending in federal court, and
For at least 30 days after dismissal
State law may provide a longer period.
A federal court dismisses the only federal claim and declines to retain a related state claim.
The plaintiff generally receives at least 30 days after dismissal to refile the state claim in state court.
Dismissed supplemental claim usually gets a 30-day refiling cushion.

sections.title: Removal
body: Removal moves a lawsuit from state court to federal court.
A defendant may remove if the federal court would have had original jurisdiction over the plaintiff’s complaint.
The case must be removed to the federal district embracing the state court where the action is pending.
A counterclaim defendant who originally filed the case generally cannot remove.
A plaintiff files a federal civil-rights claim in state court.
The defendant may remove the action to the proper federal district court.
Removal is the defendant’s elevator from state court to federal court.

sections.title: Removal Based on Diversity
body: More than $75,000 must be in controversy
No properly joined and served defendant may be a citizen of the forum state

If the case was not initially removable and later becomes removable based on diversity, removal is generally barred more than one year after commencement unless the plaintiff acted in bad faith to prevent removal
A defendant sued in the defendant’s own state cannot remove solely on diversity grounds.
A citizen of Oregon sues a citizen of California in California state court.
Even though the parties are diverse, the California defendant generally cannot remove based only on diversity.
A home-state defendant does not need diversity protection from its own state court.

sections.title: Removal Timing
body: A defendant generally must file a notice of removal within 30 days after receiving the initial pleading or summons.
If the case was not initially removable, the defendant may remove within 30 days after receiving a paper showing that it has become removable.
Six months later, the plaintiff serves a discovery response stating that damages exceed $200,000.
The 30-day removal period may begin when the defendant receives that response.
Thirty days from the paper that first reveals removability.

sections.title: Unanimity in Removal
body: All properly joined and served defendants generally must join or consent to removal.
A later-served defendant receives its own 30-day period.
Earlier-served defendants may consent to the later defendant’s timely removal.
Ordinary removal requires all served defendants on board.

sections.title: Procedure After Removal
body: After removal, the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern the case.
The parties generally do not need to refile pleadings already filed in state court.
Orders entered before removal remain effective until the federal court modifies or dissolves them.
If a defendant was not properly served before removal, federal process may be issued or service may be completed after removal.
A defendant who has not answered generally must respond within the longest applicable period under Rule 81, which commonly includes at least seven days after the notice of removal is filed.
A party that properly demanded a jury before removal need not demand again.
When state law did not require an express demand, the federal court may order one.
In other situations, short federal deadlines apply after removal or service of the removal notice.
A state judge entered a temporary discovery order before removal.
The order does not vanish when the case reaches federal court, but the federal judge may modify it.
Removal changes the court and procedural system, but it does not erase everything already done.

sections.title: Special Removal Rules
body: Claims That May Have Special Removal Rights
Federal statutes create special removal rules for matters such as:
Suits against federal officers for acts under color of office
Certain statutes restrict removal of matters such as:
Later Dismissal of the Federal Claim
If removal was proper because the complaint contained a federal claim, later dismissal or amendment of that claim does not retroactively make removal improper.
The court usually decides under supplemental-jurisdiction principles whether to retain or remand the remaining state claims.
A complaint contains one federal claim and two related state claims.
After proper removal, the federal claim is dismissed on the merits.
The judge may retain the related state claims or decline supplemental jurisdiction; remand is not automatically required merely because the federal claim disappeared later.
Test removal when it occurs; later events usually affect retention, not the original removal.

sections.title: Remand
body: Remand sends a removed case back to state court.
A motion based on lack of subject matter jurisdiction may be raised at any time before final judgment.
The federal court must remand if jurisdiction is absent.
A motion based on a procedural removal defect must generally be made within 30 days after removal.
The defendant removes to the wrong federal district.
That is generally a procedural defect.
The plaintiff must timely seek remand.
Jurisdictional remand anytime; procedural remand within 30 days.

sections.title: Personal Jurisdiction
body: Personal jurisdiction is the court’s power over the defendant or the defendant’s property.
Subject matter jurisdiction is power over the type of case; personal jurisdiction is power over the defendant.

blocks.text: Personal jurisdiction is the court’s power over the defendant or the defendant’s property.

sections.title: Traditional Bases of Personal Jurisdiction
body: A court generally has personal jurisdiction when the defendant:
Is domiciled in the forum state
Is voluntarily present and personally served in the forum
A New Hampshire domiciliary is temporarily traveling in Texas.
New Hampshire may still exercise personal jurisdiction over that person based on domicile.
Domicile, tag service, consent, or waiver can establish jurisdiction without a minimum-contacts fight.

sections.title: Tag Jurisdiction
body: An individual voluntarily present in the forum state may generally be served there and subjected to personal jurisdiction.
Tag service may be invalid if:
The defendant was fraudulently induced to enter the state for service
The defendant entered solely to participate in another judicial proceeding and is immune under forum law
A defendant visits Florida for a vacation and is personally served at a hotel.
Voluntary presence plus personal service usually equals jurisdiction.

sections.title: Consent to Personal Jurisdiction
body: Appointment of an agent for service
Consent may arise from conduct, such as using state roads under a nonresident-motorist statute.
A software agreement validly requires litigation in Virginia.
A party who accepted the clause may have consented to Virginia personal jurisdiction.
Unlike subject matter jurisdiction, personal jurisdiction may be agreed to.

sections.title: Waiver of Personal Jurisdiction
body: A defendant waives personal-jurisdiction objections by failing to raise them in:
The answer, if no Rule 12 motion is filed
The same waiver rule applies to:
A defendant files a motion to dismiss only for failure to state a claim.
After losing, the defendant tries to argue lack of personal jurisdiction.
Personal jurisdiction belongs in the first defensive response or it disappears.

sections.title: Long-Arm Statutes
body: A long-arm statute authorizes state courts to exercise jurisdiction over certain out-of-state defendants.
Statutes extending to the full constitutional limit
Statutes listing specific acts, such as doing business, committing a tort, or owning property
Does the long-arm statute authorize jurisdiction?

sections.title: Minimum Contacts
body: A nonresident defendant must have minimum contacts with the forum so that jurisdiction is consistent with fair play and substantial justice.
The contact must result from the defendant’s own purposeful conduct.
The defendant must deliberately reach into, benefit from, or create a meaningful connection with the forum.
The relevant foreseeability is whether the defendant should reasonably anticipate being sued there.
A Colorado seller repeatedly advertises to Nebraska customers, ships products there, and provides Nebraska warranty service.
The seller has purposefully availed itself of Nebraska.
The defendant must choose the forum connection; the plaintiff cannot create it alone.

sections.title: Specific Personal Jurisdiction
body: Specific jurisdiction applies when the lawsuit arises out of or relates to the defendant’s forum contacts.
Claim arises out of or relates to the contact
A bicycle manufacturer markets and sells the same bicycle model in Wisconsin.
A Wisconsin resident is injured there by that model.
Wisconsin may have specific jurisdiction even if the particular bicycle was purchased elsewhere.
Specific jurisdiction = forum contact connected to this lawsuit.

sections.title: General Personal Jurisdiction
body: General jurisdiction allows a defendant to be sued in the forum for any claim, even one unrelated to forum activity.
An individual is generally subject to general jurisdiction where domiciled.
A corporation is generally at home in:
Only an exceptional case makes a corporation at home elsewhere.
A corporation incorporated in Delaware with headquarters in Illinois is ordinarily subject to general jurisdiction in Delaware and Illinois.
General jurisdiction lives where the defendant is at home.

sections.title: Fair Play and Substantial Justice
body: Even after minimum contacts exist, the court considers fairness.
Shared policy interests of the states
Once purposeful minimum contacts are strong, the defendant must usually show a compelling fairness problem.
Contacts open the door; fairness asks whether walking through is reasonable.

sections.title: Stream of Commerce
body: A manufacturer may place a product into a distribution chain knowing that it could reach many states.
The Supreme Court has not adopted one clear test.
Some justices require only awareness that products will reach the forum.
Others require additional conduct aimed at the forum, such as:
Look for deliberate forum targeting, not merely the product’s accidental arrival.
A product drifting into a state is weaker than a manufacturer steering business there.

sections.title: Contracts and Personal Jurisdiction
body: A contract with a forum resident does not automatically create jurisdiction.
A Nevada company negotiates a five-year service agreement with a Michigan company, sends regular payments to Michigan, and chooses Michigan law.
Those facts support purposeful availment of Michigan.
One signature is not enough; examine the entire relationship.

sections.title: Internet Contacts
body: Merely operating a passive website viewable everywhere is usually insufficient.
Jurisdiction becomes more likely when the site:
Is central to the defendant’s business
A retailer’s website processes hundreds of orders from Connecticut residents and runs Connecticut-specific advertisements.
Those contacts support jurisdiction more strongly than a passive informational page.
A website seen in the state is weak; a website doing business in the state is stronger.

sections.title: Intentional Torts and the Effects Test
body: Intentional Torts and the Effects Test
A forum may have jurisdiction over an intentional tort committed elsewhere when the defendant:
Expressly aimed the conduct at the forum
Knew the harmful effects would be felt there
A defendant outside New Jersey sends false statements directly to a New Jersey employer to cause a New Jersey employee to be fired.
New Jersey may have specific jurisdiction.
Intentional harm aimed at the forum may create jurisdiction where the blow lands.

sections.title: Imputed Contacts
body: An employee’s forum contacts may be imputed to the employer when the employee acts within the scope of employment.
A partner’s business contacts may establish jurisdiction over the partnership.
A subsidiary’s contacts are not automatically attributed to the parent.
Imputation may occur if the subsidiary is the parent’s agent or alter ego.
Agency may carry contacts upward; ownership alone usually does not.

sections.title: The Federal Bulge Provision
body: A federal court may exercise jurisdiction over certain parties served within 100 miles of the courthouse, even if state law would not otherwise reach them.
The 100-mile bulge helps bring in Rule 14 and Rule 19 parties.

sections.title: Rule 4(k)(2)
body: A federal court may exercise jurisdiction when:
The claim arises under federal law
No state court has personal jurisdiction
The defendant has sufficient contacts with the United States as a whole
This rule often applies to foreign defendants with nationwide contacts but insufficient contacts with any one state.
Federal claim + no state forum + national contacts = possible Rule 4(k)(2) jurisdiction.

sections.title: In Rem Jurisdiction
body: In rem jurisdiction determines rights in property against the world.
The property must generally be located in the forum.
Interested persons must receive constitutionally adequate notice.
In rem decides who owns the thing itself.

blocks.text: In rem jurisdiction determines rights in property against the world.

sections.title: Quasi-in-Rem Jurisdiction
body: Quasi-in-rem jurisdiction determines the rights of particular parties in property located in the forum.
The judgment is generally limited to the value of the property.
The defendant’s relationship with the forum must satisfy the minimum-contacts analysis.
Property presence alone is not automatically enough when the dispute is unrelated to the property.
Property cannot replace minimum contacts when the property has nothing to do with the dispute.

sections.title: Notice and Opportunity to Be Heard
body: Notice and Opportunity to Be Heard
Due process requires notice reasonably calculated to inform interested parties and give them an opportunity to object.
Publication alone is usually insufficient for a known person whose address is reasonably available.
A county knows a property owner’s mailing address but gives notice of a tax sale only through a small newspaper advertisement.
The notice may violate due process.
Use a method reasonably likely to reach the person, not merely a ritual.

sections.title: Challenging Personal Jurisdiction and Collateral Attack
body: Challenging Personal Jurisdiction and Collateral Attack
Federal practice does not require a traditional special appearance.
A defendant may appear and raise personal jurisdiction in a timely Rule 12 response without consenting to the merits.
Continue defending and preserve the issue for appeal, or
In some circumstances, default and later challenge enforcement
The second option is risky because the defendant may challenge only jurisdiction, not the merits.
A defendant who never appeared may later resist enforcement by arguing that the first court lacked personal jurisdiction.
If the jurisdictional issue was fully and fairly litigated in the first action, it generally cannot be relitigated.
A nonresident entering a state solely to participate in another judicial proceeding may receive temporary immunity from service under forum law.
A defendant timely challenges personal jurisdiction, loses, and then litigates the merits.
The defendant may preserve the jurisdictional issue for appeal without waiving it by continuing to defend.
Raise personal jurisdiction immediately; once actually litigated, the decision usually follows the judgment.

sections.title: Property Seizure and Opportunity to Be Heard
body: Property Seizure and Opportunity to Be Heard
Before the government or a litigant seizes property through prejudgment process, due process ordinarily requires meaningful notice and an opportunity to be heard.
Temporary seizure before a hearing may be allowed when safeguards reduce the risk of error, such as:
A creditor causes the sheriff to seize a restaurant’s equipment based only on a clerk-issued form and without judicial review or a prompt hearing.
The procedure may violate due process.
Taking property first and hearing later requires strong safeguards and genuine urgency.

body: When an exception to the Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act permits suit, personal jurisdiction generally exists if:
The federal court has subject matter jurisdiction under the Act, and
The ordinary state minimum-contacts analysis does not control in the same way.
For a foreign sovereign, follow the FSIA jurisdiction and service framework.

sections.title: Venue
body: Venue determines the proper federal judicial district.
A district where any defendant resides, if all defendants reside in the same state
A district where a substantial part of the events or omissions occurred, or where a substantial part of the property is located
If neither applies, any district where a defendant is subject to personal jurisdiction
Venue follows defendants or substantial events.

sections.title: Residence for Venue
body: An entity defendant generally resides in any district where it is subject to personal jurisdiction for the action.
For a corporation in a state with multiple federal districts, analyze contacts as though each district were a separate state.
Venue residence is not always the same as diversity citizenship.

sections.title: Special Venue Rules and Defendants Outside the United States
body: Special Venue Rules and Defendants Outside the United States
Historically, a local action concerning title or possession of land had to be brought where the land was located.
Most personal claims are transitory and follow the federal venue statute.
A defendant who does not reside in the United States may generally be sued in any federal judicial district.
That defendant’s presence is disregarded when determining venue for other defendants.
Special venue statutes may govern particular categories, including:
A foreign manufacturer with no U.S. residence is joined with a domestic distributor.
The foreign manufacturer does not restrict venue, but venue must still be proper as to the domestic distributor.
Use the general venue statute unless a specialized statute or foreign-defendant rule applies.

sections.title: Transfer When Venue Is Proper
body: A federal court may transfer a case to another federal district where the case could have been brought, or to a district agreed to by all parties.
The court considers convenience and the interest of justice.

After an ordinary convenience transfer from a proper venue, the transferee court generally applies the state-law rules, including choice-of-law rules, that the transferor court would have applied. A transfer enforcing a valid forum-selection clause is an important exception.
Proper venue transfer changes the courtroom, usually not the governing state law.

blocks.text: A federal court may transfer a case to another federal district where the case could have been brought, or to a district agreed to by all parties.
The court considers convenience and the interest of justice.

After an ordinary convenience transfer from a proper venue, the transferee court generally applies the state-law rules, including choice-of-law rules, that the transferor court would have applied. A transfer enforcing a valid forum-selection clause is an important exception.
Proper venue transfer changes the courtroom, usually not the governing state law.

sections.title: Forum-Selection Clauses
body: A valid forum-selection clause is ordinarily given controlling weight absent extraordinary circumstances.
When transfer enforces the clause, the transferee court generally applies its own choice-of-law rules rather than the transferor’s rules.
A contract validly selects federal court in North Carolina.
A party files in a proper federal district in Kentucky.
The case will usually be transferred to North Carolina.
A valid forum clause strongly points the case to the selected courthouse.

sections.title: Transfer When Venue Is Improper
body: If venue is improper, the federal court may:
Transfer it to a proper district in the interest of justice
The transferee court applies its own state-law rules.
Improper venue transfer fixes the filing mistake and does not preserve the wrong court’s law.

sections.title: Forum Non Conveniens
body: Forum non conveniens allows dismissal when a more appropriate forum is in a different judicial system, often a foreign country or state court.
The alternative forum must generally be:
Courts balance private and public interests.
Transfer within federal court; forum non conveniens when the better forum is outside it.

sections.title: The Erie Doctrine
body: A federal court sitting in diversity generally applies:
The purpose is to prevent federal diversity court from producing a materially different outcome merely because the case is federal.
Federal courtroom rules, state rights and obligations.

sections.title: Federal Rule Directly on Point
body: If a valid Federal Rule of Civil Procedure directly conflicts with state law, the federal rule applies if it is valid under the Rules Enabling Act and the Constitution.
State law requires a special method for serving a particular pleading, but a valid Federal Rule directly governs that service issue.
The federal rule generally controls in federal court.
Valid Federal Rule on point usually wins.

sections.title: No Federal Rule Directly on Point
body: No Federal Rule Directly on Point
When no federal rule or statute directly controls, ask whether ignoring state law would:
Change the outcome in a meaningful way
Cause unfair administration of the laws
Erie tries to stop litigants from gaining a different substantive result by choosing federal court.

sections.title: Commonly Substantive State Rules
body: Rules for tolling statutes of limitations
Burdens of proof tied to substantive rights
Rules defining rights, liability, and recovery are usually substantive.

sections.title: Commonly Procedural Federal Rules
body: Federal judge-jury allocation, subject to constitutional limits
Rules about how the federal lawsuit moves are usually federal.

sections.title: Federal Common Law
body: Federal courts ordinarily do not create broad federal common law merely because a dispute is in federal court.
Federal common law may govern uniquely federal interests, such as:
Certain disputes involving federal proprietary interests
Congress may displace federal common law by statute.
Two states dispute their rights to water from an interstate river.
A federal court may apply federal common-law principles because the dispute implicates uniquely federal interests between states.
Erie rejects general federal common law, but narrow federal interests still produce federal rules.

sections.title: Determining State Law
body: The federal court follows the highest state court’s interpretation.
The federal court predicts how the highest state court would rule.
The federal court may certify an unsettled question to the state’s highest court if state procedure permits.
Federal diversity court predicts, but does not invent, state law.

sections.title: Choice of Law Under Erie
body: A federal court sitting in diversity applies the choice-of-law rules of the state in which it sits.
A federal court in Pennsylvania hears a diversity contract case involving performance in Maryland.
The federal court uses Pennsylvania choice-of-law rules to decide which state’s contract law applies.
Federal diversity court borrows the forum state’s conflicts rules.

sections.title: Commencing a Federal Action
body: A federal civil action begins when the complaint is filed with the court.
Filing the complaint does not itself complete service.
Filing starts the case; service brings the defendant in.

sections.title: Service of Process
body: Any nonparty who is at least 18 years old may serve process.
Service generally must occur within 90 days after filing.
The court must extend the time for good cause and may grant a discretionary extension without good cause.
Serve summons plus complaint within 90 days.

sections.title: Serving an Individual
body: An individual may generally be served by:
Leaving papers at the person’s dwelling with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there
Delivering papers to an authorized agent
Using a method permitted by state law where the federal court sits or where service occurs
A process server leaves the summons at the defendant’s home with the defendant’s adult roommate who lives there.
Person, proper home resident, authorized agent, or valid state method.

sections.title: Serving a Business Entity
body: A corporation, partnership, or association may generally be served by delivering process to:
A person permitted under state law
Serve someone with real responsibility or authorized service power.

sections.title: Special Service Rules
body: Service generally follows the law of the state where service is made or where the federal court sits.
To serve the United States, a party generally must deliver or mail process to:
The U.S. attorney for the district, and
Additional service may be required when an agency or officer is involved.
Service abroad may be made through:
An internationally agreed method, such as the Hague Service Convention
A method reasonably calculated to give notice and permitted by foreign law
Other court-ordered means not prohibited by international agreement
Foreign entities may be served through applicable international methods or other authorized means.
Ordinary service is local; government and foreign service require extra steps.

sections.title: Waiver of Service
body: A plaintiff may request that the defendant waive formal service.
A defendant who timely waives service generally receives 60 days after the request was sent to answer instead of 21 days after service.
A domestic defendant who refuses without good cause may have to pay the costs of formal service.
Waiver saves service expense, not defenses.

sections.title: Temporary Restraining Orders
body: A temporary restraining order, or TRO, provides very short emergency relief before a preliminary-injunction hearing.
A court may issue a TRO without notice only if:
Specific facts show immediate and irreparable injury before the opponent can be heard
The movant’s attorney certifies efforts to give notice and why notice should not be required
An ex parte TRO generally lasts no more than 14 days, subject to a limited extension.
No-notice TRO requires a true emergency and lasts only briefly.

sections.title: Preliminary Injunction
body: A preliminary injunction preserves the situation while litigation continues.
Likelihood of success on the merits
Notice and an opportunity to be heard are required.
Likely win + irreparable harm + favorable balance + public interest.

sections.title: The Complaint
body: A short and plain statement of subject matter jurisdiction
A short and plain statement showing entitlement to relief
The complaint must allege enough facts to state a plausible claim.
Legal conclusions alone are not enough.
Saying only “the employer discriminated against me” may be too conclusory.
Alleging specific discriminatory statements, a termination date, and different treatment of comparable employees is more likely plausible.
Facts must push the claim from possible to plausible.

sections.title: Special Pleading Rules
body: Circumstances constituting fraud or mistake must be pleaded with particularity.
Mental states such as intent or knowledge may be alleged generally.
Special damages must be specifically stated.
Performance of conditions precedent may be alleged generally.
A denial must be made with particularity.
Fraud facts specific; mental state general.

sections.title: Demand for Relief
body: In a contested case, the court may grant appropriate relief even if the complaint requested something different.
A default judgment cannot differ in kind from or exceed the amount demanded in the pleadings.
Contested case may receive proper relief; default is capped by the complaint.

body: Failure to join a required party
SMJ, PJ, venue, process, service, claim, required party.

sections.title: Waivable Rule 12 Defenses
body: The following must be included in the first Rule 12 response:
Failure to state a claim and failure to join a required party may be raised:
In a motion for judgment on the pleadings
Lack of subject matter jurisdiction may be raised at any time.
PJ, venue, process, and service are the “use them first or lose them” defenses.

sections.title: Motion to Dismiss for Failure to State a Claim
body: Motion to Dismiss for Failure to State a Claim
The court assumes well-pleaded factual allegations are true and asks whether they state a plausible legal claim.
The court does not assume that legal conclusions are true.
If the court considers outside evidence, the motion is generally converted into a summary-judgment motion, and the parties must receive a reasonable chance to respond.
Rule 12(b)(6) tests the legal sufficiency of the pleaded facts, not whether the plaintiff can prove them.

sections.title: Motion for a More Definite Statement and Motion to Strike
body: Motion for a More Definite Statement and Motion to Strike
Motion for a More Definite Statement
A party may move for a more definite statement when a pleading is so vague or ambiguous that the party cannot reasonably prepare a response.
The motion must identify the defects and the details needed.
If the court orders clarification and the pleader does not comply within the required time, the court may strike the pleading or issue another appropriate order.
A party generally moves before responding or, if no response is allowed, within 21 days after service.
A complaint alleges that the defendant committed “many unlawful acts at various times” without identifying the transactions.
A motion for a more definite statement may be appropriate if the defendant cannot reasonably answer.
Too unclear to answer: clarify it. Improper material in a pleading: strike it.

sections.title: The Answer
body: A defendant generally must answer within 21 days after service.
If service is waived, the defendant generally has 60 days after the waiver request was sent.
A Rule 12 motion changes the answer deadline.
Answer responds fact by fact and raises defenses.

sections.title: Denials
body: A defendant must fairly respond to the substance of each allegation.
State lack of knowledge sufficient to form a belief
A failure to deny an allegation generally admits it, except an allegation concerning damages.
Silence may admit liability facts, but not the amount of damages.

sections.title: Affirmative Defenses
body: An affirmative defense accepts that the plaintiff may prove the basic claim but adds a reason the defendant should not be liable.
Affirmative defenses generally must be pleaded in the answer.
An affirmative defense says, “Even if that happened, I still win because…”

sections.title: Reply and Court-Ordered Pleadings
body: A reply to an answer is not automatically required.
A party must reply only when the court orders one.
When no responsive pleading is required, allegations in the pleading are generally treated as denied or avoided.
A defendant files an answer containing affirmative defenses.
The plaintiff does not need to file a reply unless the court directs the plaintiff to do so.
Complaint gets an answer; an answer gets a reply only by court order.

sections.title: Amendments as a Matter of Course
body: Amendments as a Matter of Course
A party may amend once as a matter of course within:
21 days after serving the pleading, or
If a responsive pleading is required, 21 days after service of the responsive pleading or certain Rule 12 motions, whichever is earlier
One free amendment within the early 21-day window.

sections.title: Amendments With Permission
body: After the automatic amendment period, a party may amend with:
The court should freely give leave when justice requires.
Amendments are favored unless delay, prejudice, bad faith, or futility makes them unfair.

sections.title: Relation Back of Amendments
body: An amendment relates back when the new claim arises from the same conduct, transaction, or occurrence described in the original pleading.
An amendment changing the defendant generally relates back if:
The claim arises from the same transaction or occurrence
Within the Rule 4(m) service period, the new defendant received notice and will not be prejudiced
The new defendant knew or should have known it would have been named but for a mistake concerning identity
Same event plus timely notice can carry an amendment back to the original filing date.

sections.title: Supplemental Pleadings
body: A supplemental pleading adds events that occurred after the original pleading was filed.
Amendment: changes allegations about existing or earlier events
Amendment fixes the past; supplement adds what happened later.

sections.title: Rule 11
body: An attorney or unrepresented party must sign filings and certify that:
The filing is not for an improper purpose
Legal arguments are warranted or supported by a nonfrivolous argument for changing the law
Factual contentions have or likely will have evidentiary support
Rule 11 generally does not apply to discovery papers.
A signature promises proper purpose, legal support, and factual basis.

sections.title: Rule 11 Sanctions and Safe Harbor
body: A sanctions motion must be served but not immediately filed.
The challenged party receives 21 days to withdraw or correct the filing.
The court may issue a show-cause order.
The safe harbor does not apply.
Sanctions should deter misconduct, not merely compensate.
Serve, wait 21 days, then file if the paper is not corrected.

sections.title: Real Party in Interest and Capacity
body: Real Party in Interest and Capacity
An action must be prosecuted by the person who possesses the substantive right being asserted.
Certain representatives may sue in their own names, including:
Contracting parties authorized to sue for another’s benefit
Before dismissing for the wrong plaintiff, the court generally must allow reasonable time for ratification, joinder, or substitution.
Other entity: law of the forum state, subject to federal exceptions
A trustee mistakenly files a trust claim in the beneficiary’s name.
Before dismissing, the court should generally allow the proper party to ratify, join, or substitute.
Standing asks whether there is a judicial injury; real party in interest asks who owns the claim.

sections.title: Permissive Joinder of Parties
body: Multiple plaintiffs or defendants may join if:
Their claims arise from the same transaction, occurrence, or series of transactions or occurrences
At least one common question of law or fact exists
Subject matter jurisdiction and personal jurisdiction must still be satisfied.
Four residents are injured by the same apartment-building fire and sue the building owner.
Their claims arise from the same occurrence and share common factual issues.
Same factual episode + one common issue = permissive joinder.

sections.title: Required Parties Under Rule 19
body: Step One: Is the Absentee Required?
A person may be required if:
Complete relief cannot be given among existing parties without the person
The person claims an interest that may be impaired
Existing parties face a substantial risk of inconsistent obligations
Step Two: Can the Person Be Joined?
Joinder must be feasible, including subject matter jurisdiction and personal jurisdiction.
Step Three: If Joinder Is Impossible
Dismiss because the person is indispensable

body: Prejudice to the absent or existing parties
Whether relief can reduce the prejudice
Whether a judgment without the person would be adequate
Whether the plaintiff has another adequate remedy if dismissed
If the missing person cannot join, ask whether fairness permits the case to continue.

sections.title: Intervention as of Right
body: A nonparty may intervene as of right when:
The applicant has an interest in the property or transaction
Existing parties do not adequately represent it
A federal statute may also grant an unconditional right to intervene.
Strong interest + threatened impairment + inadequate representation = intervention of right.

body: The court may permit intervention when:
A federal statute gives a conditional right, or
The applicant’s claim or defense shares a common question of law or fact
The court considers delay and prejudice.
Permissive intervention is discretionary and depends on common issues and fairness.

body: Interpleader protects a stakeholder facing competing claims to the same property or obligation.
Rule interpleader requires an ordinary basis for federal subject matter jurisdiction.
Complete diversity must exist between the stakeholder and claimants
Ordinary personal jurisdiction and venue rules apply
Rule interpleader uses ordinary federal jurisdiction rules.

body: Minimal diversity between at least two adverse claimants
Deposit of the property or an appropriate bond
Statutory interpleader is easier: $500, minimal diversity, deposit.

sections.title: Joinder of Claims Under Rule 18
body: A party may join as many claims as it has against an opposing party.
Each claim still needs subject matter jurisdiction, either independently or through supplemental jurisdiction.
Once parties are properly opposed, Rule 18 opens the claim warehouse.

sections.title: Compulsory Counterclaims
body: A compulsory counterclaim arises from the same transaction or occurrence as the opposing party’s claim.
It generally must be pleaded in the current action or it is lost.
A seller sues a buyer for nonpayment under a delivery contract.
The buyer’s claim that the delivered goods were defective is compulsory because it arises from the same transaction.
Same transaction counterclaim: bring it now or usually lose it.

sections.title: Exceptions to the Compulsory-Counterclaim Rule
body: A claim arising from the same transaction generally need not be pleaded as a compulsory counterclaim when:
The claim was already the subject of another pending action when the current case began
Adjudicating the counterclaim would require a person over whom the court cannot obtain jurisdiction
The opposing claim was brought through attachment or similar process and the court never obtained personal jurisdiction over the counterclaimant
Before being sued in federal court, a manufacturer already filed the same warranty claim in state court.
The manufacturer may not be required to duplicate that pending claim as a compulsory counterclaim.
Same-transaction claims are compulsory unless another pending case or jurisdictional obstacle excuses them.

sections.title: Permissive Counterclaims
body: A permissive counterclaim does not arise from the same transaction or occurrence.
It may be brought now or later.
It needs its own basis for subject matter jurisdiction.
A homeowner sues a contractor over a renovation.
The contractor has an unrelated claim against the homeowner from a separate car accident.
Unrelated counterclaim is optional and needs its own jurisdiction.

sections.title: Crossclaims
body: A crossclaim is a claim against a coparty.
A crossclaim must arise from the same transaction or occurrence as the original action or relate to property involved in the action.
Two defendants are sued after a warehouse collapse.
One defendant claims the other must indemnify it for any liability.
Crossclaim goes sideways against someone already on your side of the caption.

sections.title: Impleader
body: Impleader allows a defending party to bring in a third party who may be liable to the defending party for all or part of the plaintiff’s claim.
The third-party claim must be derivative.
A defendant may implead within 14 days after serving the answer without leave of court.
After that, court permission is required.
A retailer is sued for injuries caused by a defective appliance.
The retailer impleads the manufacturer, claiming contractual indemnity.
Impleader says, “If I owe the plaintiff, this third party owes me.”

sections.title: Class Action Requirements
body: Too many to join, common issue, typical representative, adequate protection.

blocks.text: Too many to join, common issue, typical representative, adequate protection.

sections.title: Rule 23(b) Class Types
body: Separate lawsuits risk incompatible standards or impairment of class members’ interests.
The defendant acted on grounds generally applicable to the class, making classwide injunctive or declaratory relief appropriate.
Common questions predominate, and the class action is superior to individual litigation.
Risk class, injunction class, or predominance-and-superiority damages class.

sections.title: Notice and Opt-Out Rights
body: For a damages class under Rule 23(b)(3), class members must receive the best practicable notice.
Rule 23(b)(1) and (b)(2) classes generally do not provide the same mandatory opt-out right.
Damages class gets notice and an exit door.

sections.title: Class Action Fairness Act
body: Certain large class actions may enter federal court when:
The aggregate amount in controversy exceeds $5 million
The proposed class has at least 100 members
CAFA = 100 members, over $5 million, minimal diversity.

sections.title: Class Certification, Settlement, and Appeal
body: The court must determine whether to certify the class at an early practicable time.
The court may later alter or amend certification before final judgment.
A certified class action may be settled, voluntarily dismissed, or compromised only with court approval.
The court considers whether the proposal is fair, reasonable, and adequate.
Class members must receive appropriate notice.
A court of appeals may permit an interlocutory appeal from an order granting or denying class certification.
A petition generally must be filed within 14 days after the order.
A court certifies a nationwide consumer class.
The defendant may petition the court of appeals within 14 days, but the appeal is discretionary and does not automatically stay district-court proceedings.
The judge controls certification and settlement; Rule 23(f) offers a short, discretionary appeal window.

sections.title: Shareholder Derivative Actions
body: A shareholder sues on behalf of the corporation for harm suffered by the corporation.
Have owned shares when the challenged conduct occurred
Make the required demand on directors or explain why demand is excused under applicable law
Maintain ownership while the action proceeds
Recovery generally goes to the corporation.
Derivative suit belongs economically to the corporation, even though a shareholder files it.

sections.title: Initial Disclosures
body: Without waiting for a discovery request, parties generally disclose:
Individuals likely to have discoverable information
Documents and tangible things supporting claims or defenses
Insurance agreements that may satisfy a judgment
Initial disclosures generally occur within 14 days after the Rule 26(f) conference unless changed by rule, stipulation, or court order.
At the start, identify witnesses, evidence, damages, and insurance.

sections.title: Expert and Pretrial Disclosures
body: Testifying-expert disclosures are generally due at least 90 days before trial
Rebuttal expert disclosures are generally due within 30 days after the opposing disclosure
A specially retained expert usually provides a written report.
Certain treating professionals and other nonretained experts may provide a summary instead.
At least 30 days before trial, parties generally identify:
Deposition testimony expected to be used
Documents and exhibits expected or possibly offered
Objections generally must be served within 14 days after disclosure or may be waived, except for certain relevance and prejudice objections.
A defendant plans to call an accident-reconstruction engineer retained specifically for trial.
Unless the court sets another schedule, the defendant must disclose the expert and report at least 90 days before trial.
Experts usually disclose at 90 days; trial witnesses and exhibits at 30 days.

sections.title: Rule 26(f) Discovery Conference
body: The parties generally must confer as soon as practicable and at least 21 days before the Rule 16 scheduling conference or scheduling-order deadline.
The parties generally submit a written discovery plan within 14 days after the conference, unless the court orders otherwise.
Parties generally may not seek discovery before the Rule 26(f) conference unless a rule, stipulation, or court order permits it.
Meet first, plan discovery, then begin ordinary discovery.

sections.title: Rule 16 Scheduling and Case Management
body: The judge generally must issue a scheduling order by the earlier of:
90 days after any defendant is served, or
The order must limit the time to:
A scheduling order may be modified only for good cause and with the judge’s consent.
The final pretrial order controls the trial and may be modified only to prevent manifest injustice.
The court may sanction a party or attorney who:
Does not participate in good faith
Disobeys a scheduling or pretrial order
Counsel waits until after the amendment deadline to add a claim and offers no reason for the delay.
The liberal amendment standard does not erase the separate requirement of good cause to modify the scheduling order.
Rule 15 favors amendment, but Rule 16 deadlines require good cause once the schedule is set.

sections.title: Rule 16.1 Multidistrict Litigation
body: After cases are transferred for multidistrict litigation, the transferee court should hold an initial management conference and enter an initial management order.
Exchange of information about claim and defense bases
Rule 16.1 gives transferred mass litigation an early management plan.

sections.title: Scope of Discovery
body: Parties may discover nonprivileged information that is:
Relevant to a claim or defense, and
Proportional to the needs of the case
Discoverable information need not itself be admissible at trial.
Discovery is broad, but relevance and proportionality set the fence.

sections.title: Work-Product Doctrine
body: Documents and tangible things prepared in anticipation of litigation by or for a party or representative are protected.
May be discovered if the requesting party shows:
Inability to obtain the substantial equivalent without undue hardship
An attorney’s mental impressions, legal theories, and strategy receive near-absolute protection.
Facts may sometimes be reached; a lawyer’s thought process is heavily protected.

sections.title: Expert Discovery
body: The expert may generally be deposed.
Experts retained only for trial preparation and not expected to testify are generally protected absent exceptional circumstances.
Testifying expert is discoverable; consulting expert usually stays behind the curtain.

sections.title: Privilege and Inadvertent Disclosure
body: A party withholding material based on privilege must expressly claim the privilege and describe the material sufficiently to assess the claim.
After notice of an inadvertent production, the receiving party must promptly return, sequester, or destroy the material pending resolution.
Identify the privilege, and freeze accidentally produced material when notified.

sections.title: Protective Orders
body: For good cause, the court may protect a party or person from:
The court may limit the method, timing, place, or scope of discovery.
Protective order narrows discovery when its burden becomes abusive.

sections.title: Depositions
body: A party may generally take an oral deposition of any person.
Generally no more than 10 depositions per side
Generally one day of seven hours per deposition
A person is generally deposed only once
A notice may identify topics, and the organization must designate knowledgeable witnesses.
Party gets notice; nonparty needs subpoena.

sections.title: Interrogatories
body: Interrogatories are written questions served only on parties.
A party generally may serve no more than 25 interrogatories, including discrete subparts.
Responses are generally due within 30 days.
A responding party may identify business records when the answer can be derived from them and the burden is substantially equal.
Interrogatories are written, sworn, party-only questions.

sections.title: Requests for Production
body: A party may request documents, electronically stored information, tangible things, or entry onto land.
The request may be served on a party.
A nonparty must generally receive a subpoena.
Responses are generally due within 30 days.
The requesting party may specify a production format.
If no format is specified, production should occur in the form ordinarily maintained or another reasonably usable form.
Production reaches documents, ESI, objects, and property inspection.

sections.title: Physical and Mental Examinations
body: A court may order a physical or mental examination when:
The person’s condition is in controversy
The examinee must be a party or a person in a party’s custody or legal control.
Condition in controversy + good cause + court order.

sections.title: Requests for Admission
body: A party may ask another party to admit:
The response is generally due within 30 days.
Failure to timely deny or object generally results in admission.
An admission is binding in the current action.
Ignore a request for admission and the fact may become conclusively true.

sections.title: Subpoenas
body: A subpoena may command a person to:
A person generally cannot be required to travel more than 100 miles from where the person lives, works, or regularly transacts business in person, subject to rule-specific exceptions.
Subpoena gives discovery power over nonparties, but geography protects them.

sections.title: Using Depositions at Trial
body: A deposition may generally be used:
For any purpose against an adverse party who was the deponent or whose officer, director, managing agent, or Rule 30(b)(6) designee was deposed
When a witness is unavailable because of death, distance, illness, imprisonment, inability to subpoena, or exceptional circumstances
When another Federal Rule of Evidence permits its use
If one party offers only part of a deposition, another party may require introduction of other parts that fairness requires.
A corporation’s Rule 30(b)(6) designee gives damaging deposition testimony.
The opposing party may generally use that deposition for any purpose at trial against the corporation.
Depositions impeach everyone, and an adverse party’s deposition may usually be used substantively.

sections.title: Discovery Enforcement
body: Before moving to compel, the movant must generally confer in good faith with the opponent.
If the motion is granted, expenses may be shifted unless the resistance was substantially justified or circumstances make an award unjust.
Meet, confer, then move to compel.

sections.title: Automatic Exclusion for Failure to Disclose
body: Automatic Exclusion for Failure to Disclose
A party that fails to provide required disclosures or supplementation generally may not use the undisclosed witness or information unless the failure was:
The court may also impose fees, inform the jury, or order other sanctions.
A party that unreasonably refuses a request for admission and later loses on that fact may be required to pay the expenses of proving it.
A plaintiff hides a damages expert until the week before trial without justification.
The court may exclude the expert even without a prior order compelling disclosure.
Fail to disclose, and the evidence may be barred automatically unless the failure was justified or harmless.

sections.title: Discovery Sanctions
body: Failure to Obey a Court Order
Contempt, except for failure to submit to a physical or mental exam
Dismissal and default generally require serious or willful misconduct.
Discovery sanctions escalate from fees to losing the case.

sections.title: Electronically Stored Information
body: A party generally need not produce ESI from sources identified as not reasonably accessible because of undue burden or cost.
The court may still order production for good cause and may allocate costs.
If ESI that should have been preserved is lost because reasonable steps were not taken:
Severe sanctions require intent to deprive another party of the information
Negligent loss permits repair; intent to deprive permits the harshest sanctions.

sections.title: Voluntary Dismissal
body: A plaintiff may dismiss without a court order by filing:
A notice before the defendant serves an answer or summary-judgment motion, or
A stipulation signed by all appearing parties
The first voluntary dismissal is generally without prejudice.
The two-dismissal rule may make a second notice dismissal of the same claim operate as an adjudication on the merits.
Before answer or summary judgment, plaintiff usually gets one free exit.

sections.title: Involuntary Dismissal
body: The court may dismiss when the plaintiff fails to:
Unless stated otherwise, an involuntary dismissal generally operates on the merits.
Failure to join a required party
Failure to move the case or follow orders can end it with prejudice.

sections.title: Default and Default Judgment
body: The clerk enters default when a party fails to plead or otherwise defend.
The clerk may enter judgment only for a sum certain when rule requirements are satisfied.
Otherwise, the court enters judgment and may hold a hearing.
A default judgment cannot exceed or differ in kind from the relief demanded in the complaint.

sections.title: Setting Aside Default
body: The court may set aside an entry of default for good cause.
A final default judgment requires satisfaction of Rule 60(b).
Whether setting aside would prejudice the plaintiff
Undoing default is easier before final judgment.

blocks.text: The court may set aside an entry of default for good cause.

sections.title: Summary Judgment
body: There is no genuine dispute of material fact, and
The movant is entitled to judgment as a matter of law
The court views evidence and reasonable inferences in favor of the nonmoving party.
A fact is material if it could affect the outcome under governing law.
A dispute is genuine if a reasonable jury could decide for the nonmoving party.
No reasonable jury issue on an outcome-changing fact = summary judgment.

sections.title: Summary-Judgment Burdens
body: A defendant may point out that the plaintiff lacks evidence on an essential element.
The defendant does not always need to produce evidence disproving the claim.
The nonmoving party must identify admissible evidence showing a genuine dispute.
The party may not rely only on the pleadings.
After discovery, allegations must become evidence.

sections.title: Declaratory Judgment
body: A federal court with an independent basis for subject matter jurisdiction may declare the parties’ legal rights.
The Declaratory Judgment Act does not itself create federal jurisdiction.
The right to a jury depends on the underlying coercive claim.
If the threatened action would be legal, the parties generally retain the jury right.
An insurer seeks a declaration that a policy does not cover an accident.
The court still needs diversity or federal question jurisdiction, and the jury analysis turns on the nature of the underlying coverage dispute.
Declaratory judgment supplies a remedy, not jurisdiction.

sections.title: Right to Jury Trial
body: The Seventh Amendment preserves a jury right in federal civil actions that are analogous to common-law legal claims.
It does not create a jury right for purely equitable claims.
The jury generally decides legal issues first, and the judge then resolves equitable issues consistently with the jury’s findings.
Legal remedy usually means jury; equitable remedy usually means judge.

sections.title: Jury Demand
body: A party must demand a jury in writing no later than 14 days after the last pleading directed to the jury-triable issue.
Failure to timely demand generally waives the jury right.
A proper demand may not be withdrawn without the other parties’ consent.
Demand the jury within 14 days or risk losing it.

sections.title: Jury Size and Unanimity
body: A federal civil jury generally begins with at least 6 and no more than 12 jurors.
No verdict may be returned by fewer than 6 jurors
The court may allow alternate or remaining jurors to deliberate if rule requirements are satisfied.
Federal civil jury: 6 to 12, ordinarily unanimous.

sections.title: Jury Selection
body: Unlimited in number when a valid basis exists, such as bias.
Generally three per side in federal civil cases.
They may not be used in a racially or sexually discriminatory manner.
Cause challenges need a reason; peremptories need no case-specific reason but cannot discriminate.

sections.title: Jury Instructions
body: A party must timely object to an instruction or failure to instruct, stating the specific grounds.
Failure to object generally waives appellate review.
A court may correct plain error affecting substantial rights.
Specific objection before the jury retires preserves the instruction issue.

sections.title: Jury Verdicts
body: The jury announces who wins and sometimes the damages.
The jury answers specific factual questions.
The jury gives an overall verdict and answers written questions.
If answers conflict with the general verdict, the court may:
Enter judgment consistent with the answers
Special questions reveal how the jury reached the result.
