sections.title: Bench Trials and Partial Findings
body: When no jury right exists or the jury right is waived, the judge decides the facts and law.
The court must state findings of fact and conclusions of law.

During a bench trial, after a party has been fully heard on an issue, the judge may enter judgment against that party on a claim or defense that can be resolved only with a favorable finding on that issue.
Unlike JMOL, the judge may weigh evidence and credibility.
In a bench trial, the plaintiff finishes presenting evidence but offers no proof of causation.
The judge may enter judgment on partial findings without waiting for the defense case.
In a bench trial, the judge finds facts and may weigh evidence midtrial.

sections.title: Consolidation and Separate Trials
body: When actions share a common question of law or fact, the court may:
Join matters for hearing or trial
Issue orders avoiding unnecessary cost or delay
Consolidation does not automatically merge separate actions into one for every purpose.

sections.title: Prejudgment Attachment
body: Federal courts generally use the prejudgment seizure remedies available under the law of the state where the court sits.
The remedy must also satisfy constitutional due process.
A federal diversity plaintiff seeks to attach the defendant’s equipment before judgment.
The court looks to the forum state’s attachment law and constitutional safeguards.
Federal court borrows state attachment remedies, but due process still controls.

sections.title: Judgment as a Matter of Law
body: Judgment as a Matter of Law
A party may move for JMOL after the opposing party has been fully heard and before the case goes to the jury.
The court asks whether a reasonable jury would have a legally sufficient evidentiary basis to find for the nonmoving party.
The evidence is viewed in the nonmovant’s favor.
JMOL says no reasonable jury could legally find for the other side.

sections.title: Renewed JMOL
body: A party may renew JMOL within 28 days after judgment.
The party must have made a timely pre-verdict JMOL motion on the same grounds.
The renewed motion cannot raise an entirely new ground.
No pre-verdict JMOL, no renewed JMOL after the verdict.

sections.title: Motion for New Trial
body: A party may move for a new trial within 28 days after judgment.
Verdict against the great weight of evidence
A judge considering a new trial may weigh evidence and credibility more freely than on JMOL.
JMOL replaces the verdict; new trial gives the case another round.

sections.title: Remittitur and Additur
body: The judge offers the plaintiff a choice:
Accept a reduced damages award, or
Remittitur is allowed in federal court.
The judge increases an inadequate jury award unless the defendant accepts a new trial.
Additur is unconstitutional in federal court.
Federal court may reduce a jury award conditionally, but may not increase it.

sections.title: Judgment, Costs, Attorney’s Fees, and Rule 68
body: Judgment, Costs, Attorney’s Fees, and Rule 68
A final judgment should grant the relief to which a party is entitled even if the pleadings did not demand that exact relief.
Default judgments remain limited by the demand in the pleadings.
Costs other than attorney’s fees are generally allowed to the prevailing party unless a rule, statute, or court order provides otherwise.
A claim for attorney’s fees is generally made by motion within 14 days after judgment unless substantive law requires the fees to be proved at trial.
At least 14 days before trial, a defending party may make a formal offer to allow judgment.
If the plaintiff rejects the offer and later obtains a less favorable judgment, the plaintiff must pay post-offer costs.
Rule 68 generally does not shift ordinary attorney’s fees unless the underlying statute defines attorney’s fees as costs.
The plaintiff rejects it and later wins $60,000.
The plaintiff may be responsible for qualifying costs incurred after the offer.
Rule 68 punishes a plaintiff who rejects a formal offer and does worse at trial.

sections.title: Motions to Amend Findings and Alter Judgment
body: Motions to Amend Findings and Alter Judgment
In a bench trial, a party may move within 28 days after judgment to amend or add findings and to amend the judgment accordingly.
A party may move to alter or amend a judgment within 28 days after entry.
The deadline cannot be extended under Rule 6(b).
A timely Rule 52(b) or Rule 59 motion generally suspends the ordinary appeal deadline until the motion is resolved.
After a bench judgment, the defendant believes the court omitted a necessary factual finding.
The defendant may file a Rule 52(b) motion within 28 days.
Findings or judgment need correction? The key postjudgment window is 28 days.

sections.title: Clerical Corrections Under Rule 60(a)
body: The court may correct a clerical mistake or an error arising from oversight or omission in a judgment, order, or record.
After an appeal has been docketed, correction generally requires the appellate court’s leave.
Rule 60(a) cannot be used to make a substantive change in the court’s decision.
The written judgment awards $15,000 even though the judge clearly announced and calculated $150,000.
The court may correct the transcription mistake under Rule 60(a).
Rule 60(a) fixes the paperwork, not the judge’s reasoning.

sections.title: Relief From Judgment Under Rule 60(b)
body: A court may grant relief for:
Mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect
Satisfied, released, discharged, or inequitable prospective application
Grounds 1 through 3 must be raised within one year.
All Rule 60 motions must be filed within a reasonable time.
Rule 60 fixes exceptional judgment problems; it is not a substitute for appeal.

sections.title: Final-Judgment Rule
body: A party generally may appeal only from a final judgment.
A final judgment ends the litigation on the merits and leaves nothing for the trial court except execution of judgment.
An order denying a motion to dismiss usually is not final because the case continues.
Ordinary appeal waits until the district court is finished.

sections.title: Interlocutory Appeals
body: Certain orders are immediately appealable by statute, including many orders:
A district judge may certify an order when:
It involves a controlling question of law
Substantial ground for difference of opinion exists
Immediate appeal may materially advance termination
The court of appeals has discretion whether to hear it.
Interlocutory appeal is the exception, not the ordinary path.

sections.title: Collateral-Order Doctrine
body: A narrow category of orders may be appealed immediately when the order:
Resolves an important issue separate from the merits
Would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment
Conclusive, separate, important, and too late if review waits.

sections.title: Rule 54(b) Partial Final Judgment
body: In a case involving multiple claims or parties, the district court may direct entry of final judgment as to fewer than all claims or parties if it expressly finds no just reason for delay.
Rule 54(b) can make one completed piece appealable while the rest continues.

sections.title: Mandamus and Class-Certification Appeals
body: A court of appeals may issue mandamus only in extraordinary circumstances.
No other adequate means of relief
The writ is appropriate under the circumstances
Mandamus is not a substitute for an ordinary appeal.
A court of appeals may permit an appeal from an order granting or denying class certification.
The petition generally must be filed within 14 days.
The district-court proceedings continue unless stayed.
A party dislikes an ordinary discovery order and seeks mandamus.
Because eventual appeal offers an adequate remedy and the right is not clear and indisputable, mandamus is unlikely.
Mandamus is emergency appellate supervision; Rule 23(f) is a short discretionary class-certification route.

sections.title: Notice of Appeal
body: A notice of appeal in an ordinary civil case generally must be filed within 30 days after entry of judgment.
When the United States or certain federal parties are involved, the period is generally 60 days.
Certain timely post-trial motions suspend the appeal clock.
Appeal deadline is usually 30 days, and the notice is jurisdictionally critical.

sections.title: Motions That Affect the Appeal Deadline
body: Motions That Affect the Appeal Deadline
A timely motion under certain rules generally restarts the appeal period after the court disposes of the motion.
Common examples include timely motions for:
Amended or additional findings under Rule 52(b)
Attorney’s fees when the district court extends the appeal time under the applicable rule
Altering or amending judgment under Rule 59
Rule 60 relief filed within the Rule 59 period
A party files a timely motion for a new trial 20 days after judgment.
The ordinary appeal clock generally begins after the court resolves that motion.
A timely listed posttrial motion pauses and resets the appeal clock.

body: Jury verdict sufficiency — Whether legally sufficient evidence supports verdict
Law gets fresh review; facts and discretion get deference.

sections.title: Full Faith and Credit
body: A court generally gives another state’s judgment the same preclusive effect the rendering state would give it.
A party may challenge enforcement for problems such as:
Lack of jurisdiction, unless the jurisdictional issue was already fully and fairly litigated
Lack of constitutionally adequate notice or opportunity to be heard
Judgment not final or enforceable under the rendering state’s law
The enforcing court does not retry the merits.
A valid judgment travels with the force given by the court system that issued it.

sections.title: Claim Preclusion
body: Claim preclusion, or res judicata, prevents a party from relitigating a claim that was or should have been resolved in an earlier action.
Same claimant against same defendant, or parties in privity
One transaction, one full opportunity to sue.

sections.title: Judgment on the Merits
body: Dismissal for failure to state a claim
Involuntary dismissal, unless the order says otherwise
Failure to join a required party
Procedural doorway dismissals usually do not decide the claim itself.

sections.title: Same Claim for Claim Preclusion
body: Claims are generally the same when they arise from the same transaction or series of connected transactions.
A plaintiff must usually bring all theories and remedies arising from that transaction in one action.
A customer sues a contractor for property damage caused during one renovation project.
After losing, the customer files a second suit under a different legal theory for additional damage from the same project.
Claim preclusion may bar the second action.
Changing the legal label does not create a new transaction.

sections.title: Parties, Privity, and Nonparty Claim Preclusion
body: Parties, Privity, and Nonparty Claim Preclusion
Persons in legally recognized privity with them
A person is not bound merely because the person had similar interests or knew about the lawsuit.
Preclusion may apply when the nonparty:
Had a substantive legal relationship with a party
Was adequately represented in a qualifying representative action
Assumed control of the first litigation
Acts as a proxy to relitigate for a bound party
Is bound by a valid special statutory scheme
A property owner loses a quiet-title suit and later transfers the same interest to a buyer.
The buyer may be bound through the successor relationship.
Nonparties ordinarily get their own day in court unless a recognized legal relationship connects them to the first case.

sections.title: Issue Preclusion
body: Issue preclusion, or collateral estoppel, prevents relitigation of a particular issue already decided.
Party to be bound had a full and fair opportunity to litigate
Claim preclusion blocks the whole claim; issue preclusion blocks one decided issue.

sections.title: Mutual and Nonmutual Issue Preclusion
body: Issue preclusion originally required the same parties.
A nonparty may sometimes use issue preclusion against a party who previously litigated and lost the issue.
A new defendant uses the prior decision to defeat the plaintiff.
Courts commonly allow this when fair.
A new plaintiff uses the prior decision against a defendant.
Courts have greater discretion and consider fairness.
The person being bound must have had the earlier day in court.

sections.title: Criminal Judgments and Issue Preclusion
body: A criminal conviction after trial may preclude the convicted defendant from relitigating issues necessarily decided in a later civil action.
A guilty plea often has evidentiary consequences but may not satisfy the “actually litigated” requirement for issue preclusion.
An acquittal generally does not preclude later civil litigation because:
The acquittal may not identify which issue was decided
A driver is convicted after trial of intentionally falsifying safety records.
In a later civil enforcement action, the driver may be precluded from denying an issue necessarily established by the conviction.
Trial conviction may preclude; guilty plea was not litigated; acquittal usually does not bind a civil plaintiff.

sections.title: Fairness Factors for Offensive Issue Preclusion
body: Fairness Factors for Offensive Issue Preclusion
Could the new plaintiff have joined the first case?
Did the defendant have a strong incentive to litigate the first case?
Are new procedural opportunities available that could change the result?
Offensive estoppel is allowed only when it is fair, not merely efficient.

sections.title: Final Civil Procedure Memory Sheet
body: Current-rule note: This version incorporates the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure effective December 1, 2025, including the Rule 16 and Rule 26 amendments and new Rule 16.1.
Federal question = federal issue on the face of plaintiff’s well-pleaded complaint
Corporation citizenship = incorporation + principal place of business
LLC/partnership citizenship = citizenship of every member
Supplemental jurisdiction = common nucleus of operative fact
Removal = defendant moves qualifying state case to federal court
Remand = federal court sends removed case back
Traditional bases = domicile, tag service, consent, waiver
Specific jurisdiction = claim connected to forum contacts
Corporation at home = incorporation + principal place of business
Fairness = burden, forum interest, efficiency, plaintiff interest
Long-arm statute = state authorization to reach nonresident
Venue = defendant residence or substantial events
Forum non conveniens = better forum outside federal system
Federal Rule on point = generally apply valid Federal Rule
Choice of law = use forum state’s choice-of-law rules
Rule 12 waivable defenses = PJ, venue, process, service
Answer = admit, deny, affirmative defenses, counterclaims
Amendment = one early amendment as of right
Relation back = same occurrence and timely notice
Rule 11 = proper purpose, legal support, factual support
Interpleader = competing claims to one stake
Compulsory counterclaim = same transaction; bring now
Crossclaim = coparty claim from same occurrence
Class action = numerosity, commonality, typicality, adequacy
Work product = prepared in anticipation of litigation
Physical exam = condition in controversy + good cause + order
Sanctions = fees through default or dismissal
Lost ESI = severe sanctions require intent to deprive
Summary judgment = no genuine dispute of material fact
JMOL = no reasonable jury could find otherwise
New trial = serious error or verdict against great weight
Additur = not allowed in federal court
Final-judgment rule = usually wait until case ends
Claim preclusion = same transaction cannot be sued again
Issue preclusion = actually decided essential issue cannot be relitigated
Full faith and credit = enforce judgment with rendering state’s effect
